--- SOURCE 3: Biopharmaceutical ---
URL: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Biopharmaceutical

SUMMARY:
A **biopharmaceutical**, also known as a **biological medical product**, or **biologic**, is any pharmaceutical drug product manufactured in, extracted from, or semisynthesized from biological sources. Different from totally synthesized pharmaceuticals, they include vaccines, whole blood, blood components, allergenics, somatic cells, gene therapies, tissues "Tissue (biology)"), recombinant therapeutic protein, and living medicines used in cell therapy. Some regulatory agencies use the terms _biological medicinal products_ or **therapeutic biological product** to refer specifically to engineered macromolecular products like protein- and nucleic acid-based drugs, distinguishing them from products like blood, blood components, or vaccines, which are usually extracted directly from a biological source.**Biopharmaceutics** is pharmaceutics that works with biopharmaceuticals.

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Drug made from biological source

"Biologics" redirects here. For the journal, see [Biologics (journal)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Biologics_(journal) "Biologics (journal)").

A **biopharmaceutical**, also known as a **biological medical product**,[[1]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Biopharmaceutical#cite_note-1) or **biologic**, is any [pharmaceutical drug](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pharmaceutical_drug "Pharmaceutical drug") product manufactured in, extracted from, or [semisynthesized](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Semisynthesis "Semisynthesis") from [biological](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Biology "Biology") sources. Different from [totally synthesized](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Total_synthesis "Total synthesis") pharmaceuticals, they include [vaccines](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Vaccine "Vaccine"), [whole blood](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Whole_blood "Whole blood"), blood components, [allergenics](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Allergenic "Allergenic"), [somatic cells](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Somatic_cell "Somatic cell"), [gene therapies](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gene_therapy "Gene therapy"), [tissues](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tissue_(biology) "Tissue (biology)"), [recombinant therapeutic protein](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Recombinant_proteins "Recombinant proteins"), and [living medicines](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Living_medicine "Living medicine") used in [cell therapy](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cell_therapy "Cell therapy"). Biopharmaceuticals can be composed of [sugars](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sugar "Sugar"), [proteins](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Protein "Protein"), [nucleic acids](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nucleic_acid "Nucleic acid"), or complex combinations of these substances, or may be living cells or tissues. They (or their [precursors](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Precursor_(chemistry) "Precursor (chemistry)") or components) are isolated from [living](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Life "Life") sources—human, animal, plant, fungal, or microbial. They can be used in both human and animal medicine.[[2]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Biopharmaceutical#cite_note-2)[[3]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Biopharmaceutical#cite_note-3)

Terminology surrounding biopharmaceuticals varies between groups and entities, with different terms referring to different subsets of therapeutics within the general biopharmaceutical category. The term **biologics** is often used more restrictively to mean biopharmaceuticals that are produced using[recombinant DNA](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Recombinant_DNA "Recombinant DNA")technology. Some [regulatory agencies](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Regulatory_agency "Regulatory agency") use the terms _biological medicinal products_ or **therapeutic biological product** to refer specifically to engineered [macromolecular](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Macromolecule "Macromolecule") products like protein- and [nucleic acid](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nucleic_acid "Nucleic acid")-based [drugs](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Drug "Drug"), distinguishing them from products like blood, blood components, or vaccines, which are usually extracted directly from a biological source.[[4]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Biopharmaceutical#cite_note-pmid18612293-4)[[5]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Biopharmaceutical#cite_note-5)[[6]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Biopharmaceutical#cite_note-6)**Biopharmaceutics** is [pharmaceutics](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pharmaceutics "Pharmaceutics") that works with biopharmaceuticals. **Biopharmacology** is the branch of [pharmacology](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pharmacology "Pharmacology") that studies biopharmaceuticals. [Specialty drugs](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Specialty_drugs "Specialty drugs"), a recent classification of pharmaceuticals, are high-cost drugs that are often biologics.[[7]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Biopharmaceutical#cite_note-ncbi_2013-7)[[8]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Biopharmaceutical#cite_note-nytimes.com_2015_07_16-8)[[9]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Biopharmaceutical#cite_note-UofW-9) The [European Medicines Agency](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/European_Medicines_Agency "European Medicines Agency") uses the term _advanced therapy medicinal products_ (ATMPs) for medicines for human use that are "based on genes, cells, or tissue engineering",[[10]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Biopharmaceutical#cite_note-EMA_CAT-10) including gene therapy medicines, somatic-cell therapy medicines, tissue-engineered medicines, and combinations thereof.[[11]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Biopharmaceutical#cite_note-EMA_ATMPs-11) Within EMA contexts, the term _advanced therapies_ refers specifically to ATMPs, although that term is rather nonspecific outside those contexts.

Gene-based and cellular biologics, for example, often are at the forefront of [biomedicine](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Biomedicine "Biomedicine") and [biomedical research](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Biomedical_research "Biomedical research"), and may be used to treat a variety of medical conditions for which no other treatments are available.[[12]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Biopharmaceutical#cite_note-FDA_FAQ-12)

Building on the market approvals and sales of recombinant virus-based biopharmaceuticals for veterinary and human medicine, the use of engineered plant viruses has been proposed to enhance crop performance and promote sustainable production.[[13]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Biopharmaceutical#cite_note-:0-13)

In some jurisdictions, biologics are regulated via different pathways from other small molecule drugs and [medical devices](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Medical_device "Medical device").[[14]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Biopharmaceutical#cite_note-pmid18958946-14)

Major classes
-------------

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Biopharmaceutical&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Major classes")]

[![Image 4](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1d/FreshFrozenPlasma.JPG/120px-FreshFrozenPlasma.JPG)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:FreshFrozenPlasma.JPG)

[Blood plasma](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Blood_plasma "Blood plasma") is a type of biopharmaceutical directly extracted from living systems.

### Extracted from living systems

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Biopharmaceutical&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Extracted from living systems")]

Some of the oldest forms of biologics are extracted from the bodies of animals, and other humans especially. Important biologics include:[_[citation needed](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation\_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")_]

*   [Whole blood](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Whole_blood "Whole blood") and other blood components
*   [Organ transplantation](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Organ_transplantation "Organ transplantation") and [tissue transplants](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tissue_transplant "Tissue transplant")
*   [Stem-cell therapy](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Stem-cell_therapy "Stem-cell therapy")
*   [Antibodies](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Antibody "Antibody") for [passive immunity](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Passive_immunity "Passive immunity") (e.g., to treat a [virus](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Virus "Virus") infection)
*   [Human reproductive cells](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Human_fertilization "Human fertilization")
*   Human [breast milk](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Breast_milk "Breast milk")
*   [Fecal microbiota](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fecal_microbiota_transplant "Fecal microbiota transplant")

Some biologics that were previously extracted from animals, such as insulin, are now more commonly produced by [recombinant DNA](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Recombinant_DNA "Recombinant DNA").

### Produced by recombinant DNA

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Biopharmaceutical&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Produced by recombinant DNA")]

See also: [Biologics for immunosuppression](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Biologics_for_immunosuppression "Biologics for immunosuppression")

_Biologics_ can refer to a wide range of biological products in medicine. However, in most cases, the term is used more restrictively for a class of therapeutics (either approved or in development) that are produced using biological processes involving [recombinant DNA](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Recombinant_DNA "Recombinant DNA") technology. These medications are usually one of three types:

1.   Substances that are (nearly) identical to the body's key signaling proteins. Examples are the blood-production stimulating protein [erythropoetin](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Erythropoetin "Erythropoetin"), or the growth-stimulating hormone named "[growth hormone](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Growth_hormone "Growth hormone")" or biosynthetic human [insulin](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Insulin "Insulin") and its analogues.
2.   [Monoclonal antibodies](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Monoclonal_antibody "Monoclonal antibody"). These are similar to the antibodies that the human immune system uses to fight off bacteria and viruses, but they are "custom-designed" (using [hybridoma](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hybridoma "Hybridoma") technology or other methods) and can therefore be made specifically to counteract or block any given substance in the body, or to target any specific cell type; examples of such monoclonal antibodies for use in various diseases are given in the table below.
3.   Receptor constructs ([fusion proteins](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fusion_protein "Fusion protein")), usually based on a naturally occurring receptor linked to the [immunoglobulin](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Immunoglobulin "Immunoglobulin") frame. In this case, the receptor provides the construct with detailed specificity, whereas the immunoglobulin structure imparts stability and other useful features in terms of [pharmacology](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pharmacology "Pharmacology"). Some examples are listed in the table below.

Biologics as a class of medications in this narrower sense have had a profound impact on many medical fields, primarily [rheumatology](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Rheumatology "Rheumatology") and [oncology](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Oncology "Oncology"), but also [cardiology](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cardiology "Cardiology"), [dermatology](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dermatology "Dermatology"), [gastroenterology](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gastroenterology "Gastroenterology"), [neurology](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Neurology "Neurology"), and others. In most of these disciplines, biologics have added major therapeutic options for treating many diseases, including some for which no effective therapies were available, and others where previously existing therapies were inadequate. However, the advent of biologic therapeutics has also raised complex regulatory issues (see below), and significant pharmacoeconomic concerns because the cost for biologic therapies has been dramatically higher than for conventional (pharmacological) medications. This factor has been particularly relevant since many biological medications are used to treat [chronic diseases](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Chronic_diseases "Chronic diseases"), such as rheumatoid arthritis or inflammatory bowel disease, or for the treatment of otherwise untreatable cancer during the remainder of life. The cost of treatment with a typical monoclonal antibody therapy for relatively common indications is generally in the range of €7,000–14,000 per patient per year.

Older patients who receive biologic therapy for diseases such as [rheumatoid arthritis](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Rheumatoid_arthritis "Rheumatoid arthritis"), [psoriatic arthritis](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Psoriatic_arthritis "Psoriatic arthritis"), or [ankylosing spondylitis](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ankylosing_spondylitis "Ankylosing spondylitis") are at increased risk for life-threatening infection, adverse cardiovascular events, and [malignancy](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Malignancy "Malignancy").[[15]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Biopharmaceutical#cite_note-15)

The first such substance approved for therapeutic use was biosynthetic "human" [insulin](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Insulin "Insulin") made via [recombinant DNA](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Recombinant_DNA "Recombinant DNA"). Sometimes referred to as rHI, under the [trade name](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Trade_name "Trade name")[Humulin](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Humulin "Humulin"), was developed by [Genentech](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Genentech "Genentech"), but licensed to [Eli Lilly and Company](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Eli_Lilly_and_Company "Eli Lilly and Company"), who manufactured and marketed it starting in 1982.

Major kinds of biopharmaceuticals include:

*   Blood factors ([Factor VIII](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Factor_VIII "Factor VIII") and [Factor IX](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Factor_IX "Factor IX"))
*   Thrombolytic agents ([tissue plasminogen activator](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tissue_plasminogen_activator "Tissue plasminogen activator"))
*   [Hormones](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hormone "Hormone") ([insulin](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Insulin "Insulin"), glucagon, growth hormone, gonadotrophins)
*   Haematopoietic growth factors ([Erythropoietin](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Erythropoietin "Erythropoietin"), [colony-stimulating factors](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Colony-stimulating_factor "Colony-stimulating factor"))
*   [Interferons](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Interferon "Interferon") (Interferons-α, -β, -γ)
*   [Interleukin](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Interleukin "Interleukin")-based products (Interleukin-2)
*   [Vaccines](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Vaccine "Vaccine") ([Hepatitis B](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hepatitis_B "Hepatitis B") surface [antigen](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Antigen "Antigen"))
*   [Monoclonal antibodies](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Monoclonal_antibody "Monoclonal antibody") (Various)
*   Additional products ([tumour necrosis factor](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tumour_necrosis_factor "Tumour necrosis factor"), therapeutic enzymes)

Research and development investment in new medicines by the biopharmaceutical industry stood at $65.2 billion in 2008.[[16]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Biopharmaceutical#cite_note-16) A few examples of biologics made with [recombinant DNA](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Recombinant_DNA "Recombinant DNA") technology include:

| [USAN](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/United_States_Adopted_Name "United States Adopted Name")/[INN](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/International_Nonproprietary_Name "International Nonproprietary Name") | Trade name | Indication | Technology | Mechanism of action |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |

Biologics (Biologic Medicine)

Advertisement Advertisement

Biologics (Biologic Medicine)
=============================

Biologics are medications that come from living organisms, like proteins and genes. Only living cells reproduce living organisms. Biologic drugs are harder and more expensive to produce than normal drugs. But they may one day treat previously untreatable conditions, including cancers, genetic disorders and autoimmune diseases.

Advertisement

Contents[Overview](https://my.clevelandclinic.org/health/treatments/biologics-biologic-medicine#overview)[Risks / Benefits](https://my.clevelandclinic.org/health/treatments/biologics-biologic-medicine#risks-benefits)[When To Call the Doctor](https://my.clevelandclinic.org/health/treatments/biologics-biologic-medicine#when-to-call-the-doctor)

Contents[Overview](https://my.clevelandclinic.org/health/treatments/biologics-biologic-medicine#overview)[Risks / Benefits](https://my.clevelandclinic.org/health/treatments/biologics-biologic-medicine#risks-benefits)[When To Call the Doctor](https://my.clevelandclinic.org/health/treatments/biologics-biologic-medicine#when-to-call-the-doctor)

Overview
--------

### What are biologics (biologic medication and drugs)?

Biologics are medications that come from organic life. Scientists produce biologic medications by removing organic proteins or [genetic materials](https://my.clevelandclinic.org/health/body/23064-dna-genes--chromosomes) from cellular lifeforms and, when possible, reproducing them. This means scientists might have to clone genes that reproduce the proteins they need. Or they might implant altered DNA into a living cell and then reproduce it through the cell on a large scale.

Advertisement

Cleveland Clinic is a non-profit academic medical center. Advertising on our site helps support our mission. We do not endorse non-Cleveland Clinic products or services.[Policy](https://health.clevelandclinic.org/advertising)

Biologic medications are an example of biotechnology. That’s when scientists use biology (the study of living things) to develop new products and technology to address human problems. Biologic drugs (biopharmaceuticals) often treat difficult diseases that traditional human-made drugs can’t successfully treat. But biologics are also much more complicated and expensive to make than synthetic drugs are.

### What are some examples of biologics?

Biologic medications and drugs include:

*   [Blood](https://my.clevelandclinic.org/health/body/24836-blood) and blood products (like [plasma](https://my.clevelandclinic.org/health/body/22865-plasma)) for [transfusion](https://my.clevelandclinic.org/health/treatments/14755-blood-transfusion).
*   Tissues for [transplantation](https://my.clevelandclinic.org/health/treatments/11750-organ-donation-and-transplantation), like a [skin graft](https://my.clevelandclinic.org/health/treatments/21647-skin-graft) or [bone graft](https://my.clevelandclinic.org/health/treatments/16796-bone-grafting).
*   [Vaccines](https://my.clevelandclinic.org/health/treatments/24135-vaccines) that prevent [infectious diseases](https://my.clevelandclinic.org/health/diseases/17724-infectious-diseases).
*   [Insulin](https://my.clevelandclinic.org/health/body/22601-insulin) therapy for [diabetes](https://my.clevelandclinic.org/health/diseases/7104-diabetes).
*   [Immunotherapy](https://my.clevelandclinic.org/health/treatments/11582-immunotherapy) treatments for [cancer](https://my.clevelandclinic.org/health/diseases/12194-cancer).
*   [Stem cell](https://my.clevelandclinic.org/health/body/24892-stem-cells) therapy for [blood disorders](https://my.clevelandclinic.org/health/diseases/21545-blood-disorders) and [blood cancer](https://my.clevelandclinic.org/health/diseases/22883-blood-cancer).
*   [Gene therapy](https://my.clevelandclinic.org/health/treatments/17984-gene-therapy) to treat [genetic diseases](https://my.clevelandclinic.org/health/diseases/21751-genetic-disorders).
*   [Monoclonal antibody therapy](https://my.clevelandclinic.org/health/treatments/22246-monoclonal-antibodies) (cloned [antibodies](https://my.clevelandclinic.org/health/body/22971-antibodies)).
*   Biologic [immunosuppressants](https://my.clevelandclinic.org/health/treatments/10418-immunosuppressants) for [autoimmune diseases](https://my.clevelandclinic.org/health/diseases/21624-autoimmune-diseases) and [asthma](https://my.clevelandclinic.org/health/treatments/17711-biologic-therapy-for-severe-asthma).
*   [Biologic DMARDs](https://my.clevelandclinic.org/health/drugs/22683-dmards) (disease-modifying antirheumatic drugs) for [arthritis](https://my.clevelandclinic.org/health/diseases/12061-arthritis).

Risks / Benefits
----------------

### What are the advantages of biologics in preventing and treating disease?

Some of the advantages of biologic medications are:

*   **Vaccines prevent the spread of infectious diseases**. Routine vaccinations save an estimated 3 million lives each year. Global vaccine efforts have even eliminated [some diseases](https://my.clevelandclinic.org/health/diseases/10855-smallpox). And when a global pandemic like [COVID-19](https://my.clevelandclinic.org/health/diseases/21214-coronavirus-covid-19) occurs, they’re essential to controlling the rapid spread. Vaccines are among the oldest biologics in medicine, and they’ve had a huge global impact.
*   **Blood and plasma transfusions save lives**. Blood transfusions don’t only replace critical blood loss due to [hemorrhage](https://my.clevelandclinic.org/health/symptoms/21654-hemorrhage). They can also replace diseased blood with healthier blood or plasma ([plasma exchange](https://my.clevelandclinic.org/health/treatments/24197-plasmapheresis-plasma-exchange)). Healthcare providers are using blood and blood products to treat blood disorders, blood cancers and neurological disorders by removing harmful antibodies in blood.
*   **Biologics are helping people with cancer live longer**. Biologics are at the forefront of cancer treatment innovations, including stem cell therapy, gene therapy and immunotherapy. These treatments can complement other cancer treatments to help fight the disease from multiple angles. Biologics like [G-CSF](https://my.clevelandclinic.org/health/treatments/24126-g-csf-treatment) can also help you recover from radiation and [chemotherapy](https://my.clevelandclinic.org/health/treatments/16859-chemotherapy).
*   **Biologics can treat chronic inflammatory and autoimmune diseases**. Biologics can effectively control chronic inflammation and pain, preventing the need for [corticosteroids](https://my.clevelandclinic.org/health/drugs/4812-corticosteroids). Corticosteroids can become less effective over time, and their risk of negative side effects increases the more you use them. Biologics are also more selective than non-biologic immunosuppressants. They can block specific inflammatory pathways in your immune system without blocking them all.

Advertisement

### What are the disadvantages of biologics?

Some of the disadvantages of biologic medications are:

*   **Production and regulation are difficult**. Biologics are large, complex molecules that scientists can only produce within living cells. Scientists can’t simply manufacture them like normal drugs. Production processes for biologics are much more complicated and sensitive to small changes. Scientists must thoroughly control each step to ensure the product is consistent. This means biologics take longer to bring to the market than other drugs, and they’re much more expensive.
*   **Biologics can trigger an immune response**. Biologics are more likely to raise a red flag in your [immune system](https://my.clevelandclinic.org/health/body/21196-immune-system) than normal drugs. Since biologics resemble living organisms, your immune system might mistake the medicine for an infection. It might activate to remove the medicine or build antibodies against it, making it ineffective. In some cases, biologics might trigger an allergic reaction. [Allergies](https://my.clevelandclinic.org/health/diseases/8610-allergies) usually target proteins, and many biologics are proteins.
*   **Most biologics are injections**. For the most part, biologics don’t come in pill form. Some are available as self-injections, and some are IV infusions that you get at your provider’s office. Most people don’t have reactions, but injections can cause reactions like pain, swelling or a rash. Some people get a fever or feel nauseous afterward. Most reactions are mild, and you may not have any. But if you do, or if you don’t like needles, you may be less likely to take the medicine.
*   **Immunosuppressants can make it easier for you to get infections**.Biologic immunosuppressants, like TNF inhibitors, now treat a wide range of chronic inflammatory diseases, from [rheumatoid arthritis](https://my.clevelandclinic.org/health/diseases/4924-rheumatoid-arthritis) to [inflammatory bowel disease](https://my.clevelandclinic.org/health/diseases/15587-inflammatory-bowel-disease-overview) (IBD). But immunosuppressants (whether biologic or not) have the side effect of making you more vulnerable to infections. They can also slightly raise your risk of getting certain cancers. You and your healthcare provider will weigh these and other risk factors against the potential benefits of the medicine.

When To Call the Doctor
-----------------------

### Should I ask my doctor about biologics?

Depending on your condition, your healthcare provider might suggest biologic medications right away. Or they might suggest other treatments first. Other treatments might be easier to get and treat your condition successfully. But biologics have been life-changing for people with certain severe chronic conditions that haven’t responded to other treatments. They could be the next step for you.

### A note from Cleveland Clinic

While ordinary drugs will always have a place in healthcare, biologics are, in many ways, the future of medicine. Scientists are using biotechnology to target diseases more precisely and effectively, and often with fewer side effects. Research continues to refine this technology and develop new treatments for new conditions. There’s hope that gene therapy might one day target currently uncurable diseases.

--- SOURCE 5: Definition of biological drug - NCI Dictionary of Cancer Terms ---
URL: https://www.cancer.gov/publications/dictionaries/cancer-terms/def/biological-drug

SUMMARY:
A substance that is made from a living organism or its products and is used in the prevention, diagnosis, or treatment of cancer and other diseases.

DESCRIPTION: A FDA website page that shows the difference between small molecule drugs and biological products.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

enriched_points.expanded_content: Biologics are complex medications derived from living organisms, including vaccines, blood products, and gene therapies, designed to prevent, diagnose, and treat various diseases.
Biologics play a crucial role in modern medicine, offering targeted therapies for chronic conditions, enhancing treatment outcomes for cancer, autoimmune disorders, and infectious diseases.

The biologics market is rapidly growing, driven by innovation in drug development, increasing demand for personalized medicine, and advancements in biotechnology, surpassing traditional pharmaceuticals in sales.
